seo_title: LLM-Generated Conjectures to Lean Formalizations: Automated…
meta_description: Automated polynomial inequality proving using LLMs and symbolic computation, with a focus on scalability and effectiveness.
body_html:
Automated polynomial inequality proving is a fundamental challenge in automated mathematical reasoning.

Combining LLMs and Symbolic Computation

NSPi combines the strengths of LLMs and symbolic computation for polynomial-inequality proving. The framework addresses scalability challenges with a neuro-symbolic approach. Conjectures are refined via symbolic computation to obtain an exact polynomial Sum-Of-Squares representation.

End-to-End Pipeline and Effectiveness

Experiments on challenging benchmarks with up to 10 variables. Demonstrates the effectiveness and scalability of NSPI.

Key Takeaways from Lean Certification Lean certification provides strong guarantees for polynomial inequality proving.

The proposed method addresses remaining scalability challenges in automated mathematical reasoning, providing a promising solution for the field.

source.title: From LLM-Generated Conjectures to Lean Formalizations: Automated Polynomial Inequality Proving via Sum-of-Squares Certificates
source.summary: Automated proving of polynomial inequalities is a fundamental challenge in automated mathematical reasoning, where rich algebraic structure and a rapidly growing certificate search space hinder scalability. Purely symbolic approaches provide strong guarantees but often scale poorly as the number of variables or the degree increases, due to expensive algebraic manipulations and rapidly growing intermediate expressions. In parallel, LLM-guided methods have made notable progress, particularly on competition-style inequalities with a small number of variables. To address the remaining scalability challenges, we propose NSPI, a neuro-symbolic framework that combines the complementary strengths of LLMs and symbolic computation for polynomial-inequality proving. Concretely, an LLM proposes a conjecture in the form of an approximate polynomial Sum-Of-Squares (SOS) decomposition; we refine it via symbolic computation to obtain an exact polynomial SOS representation, which directly proves the target inequality, and we further certify the proof in Lean, yielding an end-to-end pipeline from heuristic discovery to machine-checked proof. Experiments on challenging benchmarks involving polynomials with up to 10 variables demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed method.
